title: Make a mini table lamp
learnerProfile: adult beginner; curious and capable; no electronics assumed
mission: Keep yesterday's button-and-LED circuit and change only the code, so one press latches the LED on and the next turns it off. The whole trick is teaching the board to act on the moment a press begins rather than the whole time your finger is down — and to see past the few milliseconds a switch bounces as it closes.

sourceMetadata.imageAlt: Official Freenove circuit for Button and LED: a LED on GPIO 2 through a 220 ohm resistor to ground, and a push button on GPIO 13 held by 10 kilo-ohm resistors, shown as both a schematic and a breadboard photo.
sourceMetadata.licenseNote: Based on Freenove official material released under CC BY-NC-SA 3.0; preserve attribution and non-affiliation language.

parts.name: ESP32-S3 board
parts.explanation: A development board is a friendly package around a tiny computer chip. The ESP32-S3 runs your uploaded sketch, controls pins, reads sensors, and can also use USB, Wi-Fi, and Bluetooth. In this lesson, it sends the ping signal and measures the echo time.

parts.name: GPIO extension board
parts.explanation: GPIO means general-purpose input/output. These pins are connection points your code can use. The extension board makes them easier to see, reach, and wire without cramming everything onto the ESP32-S3 itself.

parts.name: LED
parts.explanation: A LED is a one-way light. It only lights when its longer leg (the +) faces the pin and its shorter leg (the −) faces ground. Too much current burns it out, so a LED always sits behind a resistor.

parts.name: 220 Ω resistor
parts.explanation: A resistor is a small component whose whole job is to limit how much current flows through a wire. Current is the flow of electricity through the circuit; a resistor throttles that flow down to a safe amount. Its strength is measured in ohms (Ω) — a larger number resists harder and lets less current through. A part like a LED wired straight to power pulls far more current than it can survive and burns out at once, so it sits in series with a resistor whose value is chosen to hold the current gentle enough to keep it alive. The coloured bands print the resistor's value, and it works the same either way round — a resistor has no positive or negative end.

parts.name: 2 × 10 kΩ resistors
parts.explanation: A pull resistor is a resistor put to a different job than limiting current: it ties an input pin to a known voltage. An input pin with nothing wired to it floats — it drifts between HIGH and LOW and reads at random. A 10 kΩ resistor holds the pin at a steady resting level. Its value is large on purpose: it pulls just firmly enough to set that resting level, yet gently enough that pressing the button — a near-zero-resistance path — easily overrules it and flips the pin the other way.

parts.name: Push button
parts.explanation: This push button has four pins in two connected pairs. Pressing it bridges the pairs and completes the circuit; releasing it breaks the connection again. It is the simplest way to hand the board a yes-or-no input.

parts.name: Jumper wires
parts.explanation: Jumper wires make temporary connections without soldering. Female-to-male wires have a socket on one end and a pin on the other, useful when a sensor module and board use different connector styles.

parts.name: Arduino IDE
parts.explanation: Arduino IDE is the desktop app that opens the sketch, compiles it, uploads it to the ESP32-S3, and shows messages from the board in Serial Monitor.

wiring.reason: The pin the code drives HIGH or LOW to hold the lamp on or off.
Completes the LED's path back to zero volts.
The pin the loop watches for the HIGH-to-LOW fall that marks a press.
Holds GPIO 13 steady at HIGH until a press pulls it LOW — so the fall is a clean edge.

coachInstructions: Confirm the learner still has the Day 4 circuit before uploading; do not re-wire unless something is broken.
Keep the day to upload-and-watch; the new material is in the sketch, not the breadboard.

Land the core idea- the loop reads GPIO 13 thousands of times a second and reads LOW through the whole hold, so a lamp must act on the one falling edge (the moment of the press) and then wait out the rest.
Explain that the LED's own driven level is the remembered on/off state, held between loop passes, and that each clean edge inverts it.
Explain bounce physically- the switch's contacts rebound for a few milliseconds as they close, so the 20 ms pause lets them settle before the recheck.
If a press flips the LED twice, that is a bounce edge getting through; check that both the debounce delay and the wait-until-release line are present.

Use the challenge to make it concrete- removing the wait-until-release line exposes the level (a hold strobes the LED); removing the debounce exposes the bounce (occasional double-flips). Have them restore both.
Keep the Arduino path primary; MicroPython is optional only if already set up.
steps: Confirm the Day 4 circuit is still wired, or rebuild it from the chart.
Keep USB unplugged until every wire matches the chart.
Press the button once and let go — the LED should latch on.
Press once more — it should turn off.

arduino.explanation: Waits out the switch's physical bounce, so the recheck below reads a settled level rather than mid-bounce noise.
Inverts the LED's own current level — on becomes off, off becomes on. That level is the state the lamp keeps between loop passes.
Holds here for the whole press, so the long LOW of your finger is consumed once. The loop reacts to the falling edge, then waits for release.

micropython.explanation: The same 20 ms pause to let the switch's bounce settle before the recheck.
Holds until you release, so the press's edge registers exactly once.

theoryModel.plainLanguage: The loop reads a steady level thousands of times a second, so a lamp must act on the edge — the moment of the press — and wait out the switch's physical bounce.
theoryModel.formula: one clean falling edge → invert the remembered state → the lamp holds

notes.title: Why a press reads LOW
notes.body: The 10 kΩ resistor ties GPIO 13 to HIGH at rest, and a press pulls it to ground. So a press is a fall from HIGH to LOW — which is exactly the edge the sketch watches for.

notes.title: How this sketch catches the edge
notes.body: It acts the instant it first reads LOW, then the wait-until-release line holds through the rest of the press. The long LOW of your hold is spent there and never counted again — one press in, one flip out.

notes.title: Why the contact bounces at all
notes.body: The two metal contacts do not settle the instant they touch; they physically rebound a few times in a couple of milliseconds, like a dropped ball. The 20 ms pause lets them come to rest before the code rechecks.

expectedOutputExample: Press once and release — the LED latches on and stays on.
Press once more — it turns off and stays off.
Each full press flips the light exactly once, cleanly.
test.successCriteria: A single press that flips twice is the switch's bounce slipping past — the 20 ms pause is what holds it back, which is exactly what the challenge lets you observe.

firstChecks: A bounce edge got through — confirm the 20 ms debounce delay and the wait-until-release line are both present.
Re-run Day 4's checks — LED direction, the resistors, GPIO 2 and 13.
The loop is following the LOW level all through the hold — you're likely on the Day 4 sketch, so upload Sketch_02.2_TableLamp.
Swap in a data-capable USB cable.

challenge: Remove the wait-until-release line to watch the loop follow the LOW level for the whole hold, then remove the debounce to catch the switch bouncing, and put both back to fix each fault.
